questions.questionText: The most common gynecological malignancy worldwide is:
questions.optionB: Cervical cancer (most common gynecological cancer in developing countries — caused by HPV 16 and 18)
questions.explanation: Cervical cancer: most common gynecological cancer in developing world (including India). HPV 16 and 18: responsible for ~70% of cervical cancers. Most common histology: squamous cell carcinoma (80-85%). Screening: Pap smear/cytology (conventional or liquid-based), HPV DNA testing, VIA (visual inspection with acetic acid — low-resource settings). HPV vaccine: quadrivalent (6,11,16,18) or 9-valent. Staging: clinical (FIGO). Treatment: early — surgery (radical hysterectomy/Wertheim), advanced — concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin). Reference: DC Dutta — Cervical Cancer.

questions.questionText: Ovarian cancer is difficult to diagnose early because:
questions.optionB: It is mostly asymptomatic in early stages, presents late (Stage III/IV in >60%), and has no effective screening test for the general population
questions.explanation: Ovarian cancer: 'silent killer' — presents late (>60% at stage III/IV) due to non-specific/absent early symptoms. Most common type: epithelial (90% — serous cystadenocarcinoma most common subtype). Tumor markers: CA-125 (epithelial — elevated but non-specific), AFP (yolk sac/endodermal sinus tumor), β-hCG (choriocarcinoma, dysgerminoma), LDH (dysgerminoma), inhibin (granulosa cell). Staging: surgical. Treatment: debulking surgery + platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel). BRCA1/2 mutation: increased risk (prophylactic oophorectomy). Reference: DC Dutta — Ovarian Cancer.

questions.questionText: Endometrial cancer is most commonly associated with:
questions.optionB: Unopposed estrogen exposure (obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, nulliparity, late menopause, estrogen-only HRT)
questions.explanation: Endometrial cancer: most common gynecological malignancy in developed countries. Type I (80%): endometrioid — estrogen-dependent (low grade, good prognosis). Risk factors: unopposed estrogen — obesity (aromatization in fat), PCOS (anovulation), nulliparity, early menarche/late menopause, tamoxifen (acts as estrogen agonist on endometrium), estrogen-only HRT. Type II (20%): serous, clear cell — non-estrogen dependent, worse prognosis. Presentation: postmenopausal bleeding (most common symptom). Diagnosis: endometrial biopsy. Staging: surgical. Treatment: TAH-BSO ± adjuvant therapy. Reference: DC Dutta — Endometrial Cancer.
